Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only standard variable assignments at the top level. There is no `pkgver()` function, no command substitutions, and no dangerous commands like `eval`, `curl`, or `wget` that would execute during sourcing. The `source` array lists an HTTP URL to the official Foxit CDN (alongside local files) and includes explicit SHA-256 checksums, so fetching and verifying sources will proceed normally. No `prepare()`, `build()`, or `package()` code executes during the requested commands (`makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo`). There is no evidence of obfuscation, backdoors, or data exfiltration in the global scope.
</details>
<summary>No dangerous top-level code; safe source fetching.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<details>
The file is a simple list of library paths to be excluded from the Foxit Reader package. It contains only comments and file paths under `lib/`. There is no executable code, no obfuscation, no network requests, and no system modification commands. This is a standard packaging configuration file used to prevent bundling system libraries. No evidence of malicious or dangerous behavior.
</details>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD follows standard AUR packaging practices for a commercial binary application. It downloads the official Foxit Reader installer from the vendor's CDN (cdn09.foxitsoftware.com), verifies checksums, extracts it with 7z, applies a patch, removes insecure RPATHs via chrpath, and strips unwanted files (like updaters, activation scripts). All operations are expected for packaging this software. There is no obfuscated code, no unexpected network requests, no exfiltration, and no execution of attacker-controlled scripts. The use of `dd` and `grep` to extract embedded 7z archives from the .run file is a legitimate extraction technique for such installers.
</details>
<summary>Standard binary packaging for Foxit Reader</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for foxitreader.patch:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The patch modifies the desktop entry and the startup script for FoxitReader.  
Changes to the `.desktop` file are cosmetic (icon name, `Exec` line) and standard.  
The `.sh` patch fixes a syntax error and adds `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` to point to bundled libraries – a routine and expected practice for self-contained Qt applications.  
No network operations, obfuscated code, unusual file access, or other signs of supply-chain compromise are present.
</details>
<summary>Routine packaging patch; no malicious indicators.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<details>
This file is a static HTML document containing the Foxit Reader End User License Agreement (EULA). It contains no executable code, no network requests to unauthorized destinations, no obfuscation, and no commands that could modify the system or exfiltrate data. The only script tag is a CloudFlare email protection script (`/cdn-cgi/scripts/5c5dd728/cloudflare-static/email-decode.min.js`), which is a standard anti-spam measure and does not constitute a security threat. There is no evidence of malicious activity in this file.
</details>
<summary>Static EULA HTML file; no security issues.</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/foxitreader.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-f1ts2fif...
Cloned repository to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-f1ts2fif
Analyzing foxitreader AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-f1ts2fif) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard sources, including a direct HTTP download from Foxit's CDN for the binary installer, an EULA file, a patch, and a list of excluded files. All `source` entries point to static, expected locations, with no use of command substitution or dynamic fetching mechanisms that could introduce remote code execution during source download. The `sha256sums` array provides fixed checksums for all sources, which are verified during source retrieval, reducing the risk of tampered or unexpected content.

There is no `pkgver()` function defined, and the version is statically set, so no dynamic version resolution occurs that could carry malicious side effects. The top-level scope contains no executable commands — only metadata and variable declarations — so sourcing the PKGBUILD during `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will not trigger unintended behavior. The potentially sensitive operations (such as `dd`, `7z`, `patch`, `chrpath`, `rm`) are confined within the `build()` and `package()` functions, which are not executed under the given flags.

Thus, running the specified `makepkg` commands involves only downloading verifiable static sources and parsing safe shell metadata, with no evidence of code injection, remote payload execution, or data exfiltration during sourcing.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious code in sourcing or source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No malicious code in sourcing or source fetch[/]

makepkg --printsrcinfo successful

[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, foxitreader-excluded_files...
LLM auditresponse for foxitreader-excluded_files:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `foxitreader-excluded_files` is a plain text list of shared library files that are excluded from packaging or bundling in the foxitreader AUR package. Its purpose appears to be ensuring that certain system libraries—commonly provided by the base system or standard libraries—are not bundled with the application, thus avoiding potential conflicts and promoting the use of system-managed versions.

The content consists solely of library paths under `/lib` and follows a clear, readable format with comments indicating the originating packages (e.g., glib2, openssl). There are no executable commands, no scripts, no network operations, and no suspicious file manipulations. It does not contain obfuscated content or any form of active code. This is consistent with standard practices in AUR packaging, where such files are used to manage library dependencies properly.

The file presents no security risk as it cannot execute code, exfiltrate data, or modify the system beyond its intended role of listing exclusions for a build or packaging process.
</details>
<summary>
Non-executable list of excluded libraries, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed foxitreader-excluded_files. Status: SAFE -- Non-executable list of excluded libraries, no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD follows standard AUR packaging practices and shows no evidence of malicious behavior. It retrieves the Foxit Reader installer from the official CDN URL specified in the source array, which is consistent with the vendor's distribution method. The build process involves extracting a self-extracting .run installer by locating the embedded 7z archive via a magic byte signature, then decompressing it with 7z. This method is unconventional but not inherently malicious, as it avoids executing untrusted code and instead extracts it transparently.

The package then applies a local patch and removes specific files related to activation, updates, and maintenance tools, which could be aimed at preventing unwanted telemetry or system modifications. It also strips insecure RPATHs from several binaries using `chrpath`, a legitimate security hardening step. All file operations are confined to the build directory and final package root, with no evidence of exfiltration, remote code execution, or unauthorized system access.

All dependencies and sources are appropriate for the application, and checksums are provided for all sources. The use of `openssl-1.0` (an older version) is a backward-compatibility choice common in binary-distributed applications and not a sign of malice. The package installs into /usr/lib/<pkgname> and creates a symlink in /usr/bin, which is standard. No obfuscated commands, unexpected network calls, or dangerous scripts are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard packaging; no malicious code found
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard packaging; no malicious code found
Reviewed all required files.
Deciding which files to review next...
Decided to review 2 additional files: foxitreader.patch, eula.html
Reviewing additional files...
Reviewing additional files...
[0/2] Reviewing foxitreader.patch...
[0/2] Reviewing foxitreader.patch, eula.html...
LLM auditresponse for eula.html:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is the Foxit Reader End User License Agreement (EULA), formatted as an HTML document. It contains standard legal text outlining the terms under which the user may use the Foxit Reader software. There is no executable code, script logic, or system commands present in this file. The content consists solely of static HTML, CSS, and licensing text.

Two email addresses in Section 3.1 and 3.2 are obfuscated using Cloudflare's email protection mechanism (`data-cfemail`), and a corresponding script (`/cdn-cgi/scripts/5c5dd728/cloudflare-static/email-decode.min.js`) is included to decode them in the browser. This is a common and legitimate practice to prevent email harvesting by bots, not a sign of malicious activity. The script is loaded from a known and expected domain (Cloudflare's CDN), and there is no indication of code injection or redirection to malicious resources.

Section 8 discusses data collection and telemetry, including the collection of file metadata such as file name, author, and document size. While this raises a privacy consideration, it is disclosed transparently in the EULA and does not constitute a supply-chain attack or hidden malicious behavior. The file itself does not perform any data exfiltration; it only states that the software may do so. As such, this is an upstream application behavior, not a compromise of the AUR package.
</details>
<summary>
Standard EULA with telemetry disclosure; no malicious code
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing foxitreader.patch...
+ Reviewed eula.html. Status: SAFE -- Standard EULA with telemetry disclosure; no malicious code
LLM auditresponse for foxitreader.patch:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided patch modifies two files: `FoxitReader.desktop` and `FoxitReader.sh`. The changes to the `.desktop` file are benign, updating metadata such as the Comment, Icon name, and adding the `Exec` line—standard practice for desktop integration. The icon name change from `FoxitReader` to `foxitreader` likely reflects a lowercase naming convention in the system's icon theme.

The modification to `FoxitReader.sh` improves script portability and environment setup. It correctly sets `selfpath` using `readlink -f` to determine the script's directory and exports `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` to include several subdirectories containing libraries and plugins (e.g., `lib`, `platforms`, `imageformats`). This ensures the bundled Qt and other components are found at runtime. While manipulating `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` can be a security concern in some contexts, here it is appropriately scoped to the application's own directory and serves the legitimate purpose of self-contained execution.

There are no signs of obfuscation, network activity, or execution of external code. All changes align with standard packaging practices for relocatable binary applications. The use of `exec` to launch the binary with arguments is normal and safe.
</details>
<summary>
Patch contains safe, standard packaging changes</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed foxitreader.patch. Status: SAFE -- Patch contains safe, standard packaging changes
